questions.question: Which decoder achieves the highest threshold for surface codes?
questions.explanation: MWPM achieves the highest practical threshold at approximately 1.0%, close to the optimal decoder threshold of ~1.1%. Union-Find is slightly lower at ~0.9%, trading accuracy for speed.

questions.question: Which decoder is best suited for real-time quantum error correction?
questions.explanation: Union-Find has near-linear time complexity O(d² α(d²)) and is highly parallelizable, making it ideal for real-time decoding. MWPM, while more accurate, has higher latency that can exceed real-time constraints.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of neural network decoders?

options.text: They always have the highest threshold
They can adapt to device-specific and correlated noise patterns
They use the least computational resources

questions.explanation: Neural network decoders can learn from actual device data, adapting to complex noise patterns including spatial correlations, crosstalk, and non-Markovian effects that traditional decoders designed for idealized models may miss.

questions.question: For very small codes (d ≤ 3), which decoder approach is optimal?
questions.explanation: For very small codes, a precomputed lookup table mapping every possible syndrome to its optimal correction is feasible and provides both optimal accuracy and O(1) lookup time. This approach doesn't scale to larger codes due to exponential table size.

questions.question: What is a hybrid decoder approach?

options.text: A decoder that only works on hybrid quantum-classical systems
Combining multiple decoding algorithms to leverage their different strengths
A decoder that uses both X and Z syndromes
A decoder implemented on hybrid hardware

questions.explanation: Hybrid decoders combine multiple algorithms—for example, using fast Union-Find for initial decoding and MWPM for refinement when uncertain, or using neural networks to predict edge weights for matching algorithms.

questions.question: Which decoder type works best for LDPC codes?
questions.explanation: Belief Propagation works particularly well for LDPC codes because they have sparse parity check matrices with fewer short loops in their factor graphs. Combined with OSD post-processing, BP can approach optimal performance for LDPC codes.

questions.question: The difference between decoder thresholds (e.g., 1.0% vs 0.9%) becomes less significant when operating well below threshold.
questions.explanation: True. When operating well below threshold (e.g., at 0.1% physical error rate), both decoders suppress logical errors very effectively. The logical error rate depends on (p/p_th)^((d+1)/2), so the difference between thresholds matters less when p << p_th.
